title: France Visa Photo Requirements (2026 Latest Guide): Size, Rules & Tips for Students, Professionals & Tourists
description: Complete 2026 guide to France Schengen visa photo requirements. Covers official ICAO size (35–40×45mm), face height (32–36mm), background rules, file format, lighting, and tips for students, professionals & tourists. Verified against france-visas.gouv.fr.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
France Visa Photo Requirements (2026 Latest Guide): Size, Rules & Tips for Students, Professionals & Tourists

Applying for a France visa — whether a short-stay Schengen tourist visa, a long-stay student visa, or a professional work permit — requires submitting a photograph that meets the official ICAO (International Civil Aviation Organisation) photo standards referenced directly by the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs on the official france-visas.gouv.fr portal.

An incorrect photograph is one of the most common — and most avoidable — reasons for visa application delays and rejections. The biometric face-recognition systems used at French consulates and VFS Global / TLScontact visa application centres are sensitive to size, face coverage, lighting, and image quality. A photo that looks perfectly normal to the human eye can still fail automated checks if it falls outside the technical specifications.

This guide covers every official France visa photo requirement for 2026, verified against france-visas.gouv.fr (the official French visa portal operated by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs) and the ICAO Doc 9303 standard that France-Visas explicitly references for all photo submissions.

France Visa Photo Requirements — Official Specifications

According to the france-visas.gouv.fr FAQ (Ministry of Foreign Affairs, France), all visa applications require photographs that comply with the ICAO standard. France-Visas specifies two identical physical photographs for in-person applications, and one digital upload for online submissions. The photo must be recent and conform to reality.

Requirement Official Standard (france-visas.gouv.fr / ICAO Doc 9303)
Photo Width35mm to 40mm
Photo Height45mm (standard ICAO format)
Face Height (chin to forehead, excluding hair)32mm to 36mm — 70% to 80% of the total image height
BackgroundPlain white or very light grey — no patterns, shadows, or objects
ExpressionNeutral — mouth closed, eyes open, looking directly at camera
Photo AgeRecent — must conform to current appearance
GlassesNot permitted under ICAO standard (referenced by france-visas.gouv.fr)
Head CoveringsNot permitted except for religious or medical reasons
ShadowsNone on face or background
Retouching / FiltersNot permitted — colour neutrality and natural skin tone required
StandardICAO Doc 9303 / ISO IEC compliant
Identical copies required2 (physical in-person); 1 digital upload (online applications)

📋 Official Source Note

The specifications above are drawn directly from the france-visas.gouv.fr FAQ and the ICAO photograph requirements document linked from the official France-Visas portal. France-Visas states: "The photo should be between 35 and 40 mm wide. The size of the face should be 32 to 36 mm (70 to 80% of the picture) from chin to forehead (excluding hair) and comply with the ICAO standard."

Photo Size — Width and Height

The official france-visas.gouv.fr FAQ specifies that the photograph must be 35mm to 40mm wide and conform to the standard ICAO portrait format of 45mm height. The most common compliant size used at French consulates and visa application centres worldwide is 35mm × 45mm, which is the standard Schengen biometric format. A width up to 40mm is also accepted, giving a small tolerance — but 35×45mm is the format to request at any photo studio.

Face Height — The Critical Measurement

This is the most precisely defined — and most commonly failed — requirement. The official france-visas.gouv.fr specification states that the face must measure 32mm to 36mm from chin to forehead (excluding hair). This corresponds to 70%–80% of the total image height. This is measured from the bottom of the chin to the top of the forehead — not to the top of the hair. A common mistake is standing too far from the camera, producing a face height below 32mm, which will fail automated biometric scanning.

Background

The background must be plain white or very light grey, uniformly lit with no shadows, patterns, textures, or other people or objects. The ICAO standard — which france-visas.gouv.fr explicitly references — requires a light, neutral background that creates clear contrast with the applicant's face. Any colour tint, shadow gradient, or visible pattern will cause rejection.

Facial Expression

Your expression must be neutral with your mouth fully closed. Eyes must be open, clearly visible, and looking directly at the camera lens. No smiling, squinting, frowning, or raised eyebrows. This requirement exists because ICAO-compliant facial recognition systems measure biometric distances between facial landmarks — altered expressions change these distances and reduce matching accuracy across border control databases.

Age of Photo

France-Visas states the photograph "must be recent and conform to reality." Standard consulate guidance across French embassies and VFS Global centres specifies a photo taken within the last 6 months, accurately reflecting your current appearance. If your appearance has significantly changed — new facial hair, different hairstyle, significant weight change — a new photo is required even within the 6-month window.

Photo Quality

The photograph must be in sharp focus, with no blur, pixelation, grain, or ink marks. Colour rendering must be natural — no oversaturation or desaturation. For physical submissions, the photo must be printed on professional photographic paper. The ICAO standard specifies even lighting without shadows, reflections, or excessive glare, with all facial features clearly visible.

Technical Specifications — Digital Submissions

File Format and Pixel Dimensions

For digital uploads via visa application portals and VFS Global / TLScontact centres, accepted file formats are JPG, JPEG, and PNG in the sRGB colour space. The minimum pixel dimensions are 400 × 600 pixels. A recommended resolution of 300 DPI or higher at the 35×45mm print dimensions — translating to approximately 413 × 531 pixels at 300 DPI — ensures the image meets biometric quality checks. Any modern smartphone (12 megapixels or above) in good lighting will produce sufficient resolution.

File Size

For digital submissions through VFS Global and TLScontact centres handling French visa applications, the maximum accepted file size is 120 KB. This is a strict technical limit — files exceeding this threshold will trigger an upload error. If your photo file is larger, export it as a compressed JPEG at high quality (not maximum) to bring it within the limit while retaining sufficient detail. The minimum pixel requirement of 400×600 pixels still applies, so do not over-compress.

⚠️ Important: File Size Limit

The digital file size limit for VFS Global and TLScontact digital submissions for French visa applications is 120 KB maximum. This is commonly misquoted as 4 MB or higher. Export your photo as a compressed JPEG and verify the file size before uploading. Files over 120 KB will be rejected at the upload stage.

Printed vs Digital Requirements

For in-person consulate and visa centre applications, two identical physical prints on photographic paper (35×45mm) are required. For online portal submissions, a single digital image upload is accepted. Both routes require identical subject-side standards — face coverage, background, expression, lighting — with only the delivery format differing. Confirm your application method through your VFS Global or TLScontact confirmation to ensure you prepare the correct format.

Lighting and Contrast

Lighting must be even and diffuse with no shadows on either the face or the background. The ICAO standard (Doc 9303) specifies that all facial features must be clearly visible with no reflections, harsh highlights, or shadow areas. Stand approximately 50cm from your background to prevent your shadow from falling on it. For applicants with very light skin tones, a pale grey background provides better biometric contrast than pure white.

Do's and Don'ts for France Visa Photos

✅ Do's

  • ✔ Use a plain white or very light grey background
  • ✔ Ensure face height is 32–36mm (chin to forehead, excluding hair)
  • ✔ Centre your face squarely in the frame
  • ✔ Keep your expression neutral — mouth closed, eyes fully open
  • ✔ Ensure even, shadow-free lighting on face and background
  • ✔ Look directly at the camera lens
  • ✔ Ensure your full face, chin, and forehead are visible
  • ✔ Use a recent photo taken within 6 months
  • ✔ Use minimum 300 DPI resolution; keep file under 120 KB for digital
  • ✔ Disable all phone beauty filters, portrait mode, and AI enhancements
  • ✔ Have both ears visible (unless covered for religious reasons)
  • ✔ Use a photo that accurately reflects your current appearance

❌ Don'ts

  • ✘ Do not wear glasses — not permitted under ICAO standard
  • ✘ Do not tilt, turn, or angle your head
  • ✘ Do not smile or alter your expression
  • ✘ Do not wear a hat, cap, or head covering (except religious/medical)
  • ✘ Do not use patterned, coloured, or textured backgrounds
  • ✘ Do not apply filters, skin smoothing, brightness edits, or AI tools
  • ✘ Do not submit a selfie — arm-length photos produce insufficient face coverage
  • ✘ Do not submit a blurry, pixelated, or low-resolution photo
  • ✘ Do not upload a digital file exceeding 120 KB
  • ✘ Do not wear sunglasses, tinted lenses, or coloured contacts
  • ✘ Do not submit a photo taken more than 6 months ago
  • ✘ Do not print on standard inkjet paper — use photographic paper only

France Visa Photo Requirements by Applicant Type

The official ICAO-compliant photo specifications are identical for all France visa categories. However, different applicant groups make predictably different mistakes. Here is what to watch for depending on your visa type.

Students — Campus France / Long-Stay Student Visa (India to France)

Indian students applying for a French long-stay student visa (type D) through Campus France represent one of the largest applicant groups. The photo requirements are identical to all other visa categories, but several errors appear repeatedly in this group:

  • Selfies: The face height achieved at arm's length is almost always below the 32mm minimum. Use a tripod or ask someone else to hold the phone, positioned at eye level, using the rear camera.
  • Beauty mode and filters left active: Many Android and iPhone camera apps apply skin smoothing, eye widening, or scene enhancements by default. These alter facial geometry in ways that fail biometric verification. Go to Camera Settings and turn off every enhancement before the shoot.
  • Old passport photos reused: Student applicants commonly reuse photos from earlier visa applications. If the photo is more than 6 months old or your appearance has changed, take a new one — consulate staff check this during in-person verification.
  • Coloured or patterned backgrounds: A white bedsheet pinned against a plain wall works well as long as it is completely crease-free and evenly lit.

Professionals — Passeport Talent / Work Visa / ICT Applicants

For Passeport Talent (talent passport) and Intra-Company Transfer (ICT) visa applicants, the photo must meet the same ICAO technical standards — professional visa applications carry no additional latitude on photo requirements. Practical considerations specific to this group:

  • Dress code: France-Visas guidance does not mandate any specific clothing. Wear something plain and neutral. A shirt or blouse in a solid, muted colour works well — it keeps visual attention on the face and avoids the automated border area flagging that high-contrast patterns near the chin can occasionally trigger.
  • Glasses: Even if you wear prescription glasses every day, remove them for the photo. France-Visas references the ICAO standard, under which glasses are not permitted. Glare and lens reflections on any lens type — including anti-reflective coated lenses — cause biometric processing failures.
  • Facial hair consistency: Ensure your appearance in the photo matches your current appearance at the time of travel. Changes in beard or moustache style after photo submission may require a new photo at the application centre.

Tourists — Short-Stay Schengen Visa (Type C)

Tourist visa applicants must meet the same photo standards as long-stay applicants — there is no simplified process for short stays. The most common error among tourist applicants is submitting a cropped holiday or travel photograph. These almost always fail on background, lighting, or face coverage grounds. A purpose-taken photo against a plain background takes under five minutes to arrange and is far more reliable than cropping an existing photo.

Children and Infants

Children of all ages applying for a France Schengen visa require an individual photograph — a child cannot appear in a parent's photo. The same ICAO-compliant 35×45mm dimensions and 32–36mm face height rules apply. Official guidance provides limited flexibility for very young children:

  • Eyes: Infants who cannot sustain a direct forward gaze are accepted with minor deviations, but the face must still be clearly visible and centred.
  • Mouth: A slightly open mouth is accepted for infants and very young toddlers who cannot hold a closed expression.
  • Support: If the infant must be supported, ensure no hands, arms, or other objects are visible in the frame. A car seat covered with a plain white cloth, photographed from directly above, is a practical solution for newborns.
  • Children under 12: France-Visas confirms that children under 12 are exempt from biometric data collection (fingerprints) at the application stage, but an ICAO-compliant photo must still be included in the application file.

Common Reasons for France Visa Photo Rejection

  1. Face height below 32mm: The most common automated rejection trigger — face occupying less than 70% of image height, usually from standing too far from the camera.
  2. Incorrect photo width or dimensions: Photo outside the 35–40mm width range, or aspect ratio not conforming to ICAO portrait format.
  3. Shadows on face or background: Poor single-source or overhead lighting creates shadows that fail biometric analysis.
  4. Non-white or non-grey background: Any colour, pattern, or texture results in rejection under ICAO standards.
  5. Photo not recent: Photo older than 6 months, or that no longer reflects current appearance.
  6. Glasses worn: Prohibited under the ICAO standard referenced by france-visas.gouv.fr — glare and reflections cause system failures.
  7. Retouching, filters, or AI editing: Skin smoothing, brightness adjustment, background replacement, and AI enhancement are all grounds for rejection — colour neutrality and natural skin tone are required.
  8. Head not straight: Tilted, turned, or chin-up/chin-down poses fail ICAO geometric alignment requirements.
  9. Low image quality: Blurry, pixelated, or low-contrast images fail automated quality screening.
  10. Digital file over 120 KB: Exceeds the upload limit for VFS Global / TLScontact digital submissions — causes upload error rather than rejection of the application itself, but prevents submission.
  11. Printed on inkjet paper: Physical copies on standard office paper are rejected at the application counter — photographic paper is required.

Glasses Policy — Official Position

France-Visas.gouv.fr explicitly states that all photos must comply with the ICAO standard. The ICAO Doc 9303 standard does not permit glasses in biometric travel document photos, as lens reflections — including those from anti-reflective coated lenses — interfere with automated facial feature detection. Remove all glasses, including prescription eyewear, for your France visa photograph.

Tips to Take a France Visa Photo at Home

You do not need a professional studio. A modern smartphone and a few minutes of preparation are sufficient to produce a fully ICAO-compliant photo.

Smartphone Setup

  • Use the rear camera, not the front-facing camera — rear cameras have higher resolution and less barrel distortion.
  • Ask someone to take the photo, or use a tripod with a 3–5 second self-timer.
  • Position the camera at eye level — not above or below — to avoid facial distortion.
  • Set camera to its highest JPEG quality setting.
  • Disable portrait mode, beauty mode, AI scene enhancement, Photographic Styles (iPhone: Settings → Camera → Photographic Styles → None), and all automatic filters. On Android, these are typically under Camera → Settings → Beauty, AI Shot, or Filters.

Background Setup

  • Use a plain white bedsheet, a sheet of white foam board, or a white wall as your backdrop.
  • Stand 50–60cm away from the background — this distance prevents your shadow from falling on it.
  • Avoid standing near coloured walls — ambient colour reflection can tint an otherwise white background under certain lighting conditions.
  • Ensure the background is crease-free and uniformly lit across its entire visible area.

Lighting

  • Natural window light is the most reliable source — face a window so light falls evenly across your face.
  • Never photograph with the window behind you — this creates a silhouette with an overexposed background.
  • For artificial lighting, use two light sources of equal intensity placed at 45-degree angles on either side of your face — this eliminates shadow.
  • Avoid overhead lighting — it reliably creates shadows under eyes, nose, and chin that automated systems flag.

Where to Get France Visa Photos

Online Photo Tools

Online tools like PixPassport allow you to upload a photo taken at home and have it automatically verified against ICAO and France Schengen specifications — checking face coverage ratio, background uniformity, file size, image sharpness, and aspect ratio before submission. This is the fastest, most cost-effective option if you have a good smartphone camera.

Photo Studios

Professional studios familiar with ICAO and Schengen biometric standards can produce compliant prints in minutes. When visiting a studio, ask specifically for a Schengen visa photo, 35×45mm, ICAO compliant — do not simply say "passport photo," as default sizes vary by country. Confirm they print on photographic paper and can produce the correct dimensions before you sit down.

When to Choose Each

Use an online tool when: applying through a digital portal, checking compliance before a studio visit, or when no professional studio is easily accessible. Use a studio when: physical prints are required, your consulate requests professionally produced photographs, or adequate home lighting is not achievable.

Specification 🇫🇷 France (Schengen / ICAO) 🇳🇿 New Zealand 🇺🇸 United States
Print size35–40mm wide × 45mm high35×45mm51×51mm (2×2 inches)
Face height32–36mm (chin to forehead, excl. hair) = 70–80%70–80% of image height50–69% of image height (ICAO base)
BackgroundWhite or very light greyNeutral and plain (no colour specified)White or off-white
Glasses❌ Not permitted (ICAO standard)✅ Allowed (conditions apply)❌ Not permitted (since 2016)
Digital file formatJPG / PNG (sRGB)JPG / JPEG onlyJPEG
Digital file sizeMax 120 KB (VFS Global / TLScontact)512 KB – 3.14 MBNot specified (varies by portal)
Min pixel dimensions400 × 600 pxNot specified (min 512 KB ensures quality)600 × 600 px
Photo ageRecent (current appearance) / 6 months6 months6 months
StandardICAO Doc 9303INZ biometric standardState Department / ICAO

France Visa Photo Checklist — Before You Submit

  • ☐ Photo is recent — taken within 6 months and reflects current appearance
  • ☐ Photo width is 35–40mm; height is 45mm (standard 35×45mm is the safest)
  • ☐ Face height (chin to forehead, excluding hair) is 32–36mm
  • ☐ Digital file is JPG or PNG in sRGB colour space
  • ☐ Digital file is under 120 KB and at least 400 × 600 pixels
  • ☐ Face is centred and looking directly at the camera
  • ☐ Expression is neutral — mouth closed, eyes fully open
  • ☐ Head is straight — no tilt, turn, or chin angle
  • ☐ Background is plain white or very light grey — no shadows, patterns, or objects
  • ☐ No glasses of any type (prescription, tinted, anti-reflective)
  • ☐ No hat or head covering (unless religious or medical)
  • ☐ No filters, AI edits, skin smoothing, beauty mode, or background replacement
  • ☐ Both ears visible (unless covered for religious reasons)
  • ☐ Photo is sharp — no blur, grain, pixelation, or compression artefacts
  • ☐ Lighting is even — no shadows under eyes, nose, or chin
  • ☐ Printed on photographic paper (if submitting physical copies)
  • ☐ Two identical copies prepared (for in-person consulate submissions)

title: India Visa Requirements for Foreigners (2026 Complete Guide): e-Visa, Types, Fees, Photos & Application Process
description: Complete 2026 guide to India visa requirements for foreign nationals. Covers e-Visa eligibility (166+ countries), types of visas, fees, photo specifications (2×2 inch/51×51mm), entry airports, FRRO registration, and step-by-step application process. Verified against indianvisaonline.gov.in, boi.gov.in, mha.gov.in and mea.gov.in.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
India Visa Requirements for Foreigners (2026 Complete Guide): e-Visa, Types, Fees, Photos & Application Process

Planning to travel to India in 2026? Whether you are visiting for tourism, business, medical treatment, or to meet family, obtaining the correct visa before arrival is a legal requirement for almost all foreign nationals. India operates one of the world's largest e-Visa systems, covering citizens of over 166 countries and territories — but the rules, fees, photo specifications, and entry requirements vary significantly by nationality and purpose of visit.

This guide is compiled exclusively from official Indian government sources: the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in), the Indian Visa Online portal (indianvisaonline.gov.in), the Ministry of Home Affairs (mha.gov.in), and the Ministry of External Affairs (mea.gov.in). Every factual claim is sourced from these portals. Visa rules can change — always verify the latest requirements at indianvisaonline.gov.in before submitting your application.

Important: Beware of Fake Visa Websites

The Government of India has not authorised any agent or intermediary to charge fees for facilitating emergency or express visas or e-Visas. The only official Indian e-Visa application portal is indianvisaonline.gov.in/evisa. Any other website charging "service fees" or "express processing" fees for Indian government visas is unofficial. Report suspicious websites to Indian immigration authorities.

Do Foreigners Need a Visa to Enter India?

According to the official Indian Visa Online portal (indianvisaonline.gov.in), all foreign nationals entering India must possess a valid international travel document in the form of a national passport along with a valid visa issued by an Indian Mission or Post, or an e-Visa granted by the Bureau of Immigration — except for nationals specifically exempted under bilateral agreements.

The following nationalities are exempt from obtaining a visa before arrival, per official sources:

  • Nepal and Bhutan: Citizens of Nepal and Bhutan do not require a visa to enter India. However, if entering India from China, Macau, Hong Kong, or Pakistan, a passport and visa are required. (Source: Bureau of Immigration, boi.gov.in)
  • Maldives: Maldivian citizens visiting India for up to 90 days for tourism are exempt from visa requirements when holding a valid Maldivian passport, per the Incredible India government portal (incredibleindia.gov.in).
  • Diplomatic and Official Passport holders: Subject to separate bilateral visa exemption agreements listed on the Ministry of External Affairs website (mea.gov.in/bvwa-menu.htm).

All other foreign nationals — including citizens of the United States, United Kingdom, European Union, Canada, Australia, and most other countries — require either an e-Visa or a regular (sticker) visa issued by an Indian Mission or Post.

What Is the India e-Visa?

The India e-Visa (Electronic Travel Authorisation or ETA) is a fully online visa system operated by the Bureau of Immigration, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India. It allows eligible foreign nationals to apply for a visa from their home country without visiting an Indian embassy or consulate. According to indianvisaonline.gov.in, the e-Visa is available for tourism, business, medical treatment, and conference purposes.

Key official facts about the India e-Visa, per the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in and indianvisaonline.gov.in):

  • The e-Visa is non-extendable and non-convertible once granted.
  • It is not valid for visiting Protected Areas, Restricted Areas, or Cantonment Areas — separate permission from civil authority is required for those zones.
  • Biometric details (fingerprints and photograph) are captured at immigration on arrival in India.
  • e-Visa holders must arrive through designated Immigration Check Posts (airports and seaports) — see the full list below.
  • The e-Visa fee is non-refundable whether the application is approved or rejected.

Types of India e-Visa — Official Categories

According to the Ministry of Home Affairs document on visa details (mha.gov.in) and the Indian e-Visa portal (indianvisaonline.gov.in), there are three main sub-categories of India e-Visa:

e-Visa Type Purpose Validity Options Entries
e-Tourist Visa Recreation, sightseeing, visiting friends/relatives, short-term yoga programmes, short courses (music, dance, cooking, language etc.) 30 days / 1 year / 5 years Double (30-day); Multiple (1-yr & 5-yr)
e-Business Visa All activities permitted under regular Business Visa 1 year Multiple
e-Medical Visa Medical treatment including Indian systems of medicine (Ayurveda, Yoga, etc.) 60 days Triple
e-Medical Attendant Visa Accompanying an e-Medical Visa holder (up to 2 attendants) Co-terminus with principal e-Medical Visa Triple
e-Conference Visa Attending conferences, seminars, workshops organised by Government of India Ministries, State Governments, PSUs, and their subordinate organisations 30 days Single

📋 Official Source Note

The above categories and entry rules are sourced directly from indianvisaonline.gov.in (Bureau of Immigration, Ministry of Home Affairs, Government of India). For maximum stay rules: the 1-year and 5-year e-Tourist Visa holders must not exceed 180 days in India during one calendar year. The 30-day e-Tourist Visa permits a stay of 30 days from the date of arrival. Always confirm current rules at indianvisaonline.gov.in/evisa/tvoa.html before applying.

Types of Regular (Sticker) Visa — Indian Mission/Post

For those not eligible for e-Visa, or whose purpose of visit falls outside e-Visa categories, a regular visa must be obtained from the Indian Mission or Post (embassy/consulate) in your country. The Ministry of Home Affairs (mha.gov.in) recognises the following principal categories of regular visa:

  • Tourist Visa (T): For recreation, sightseeing, casual visits to friends or relatives, short-term yoga programmes. Not for activities that generate income.
  • Business Visa (B): For business activities, trade visits, establishing business ventures, attending business meetings.
  • Employment Visa (E): For foreign nationals taking up employment with Indian companies or organisations. Requires a minimum salary threshold (₹16.25 lakhs per annum as per MHA guidelines). Not granted for manual or clerical work.
  • Student Visa (S): For pursuing full-time academic or research programmes at recognised Indian educational institutions.
  • Medical Visa (MED): For medical treatment at recognised/specialised hospitals in India. Requires a letter from the hospital.
  • Medical Attendant Visa (MED-X): For attendants accompanying a Medical Visa holder. Maximum of 2 attendants per patient.
  • Conference Visa (C): For attending conferences, seminars, workshops, etc.
  • Transit Visa (TR): For transiting through India to a destination outside India. Valid for a stay not exceeding 3 days for each leg of the journey. Can be issued for 2 entries.
  • Research Visa (R): For research work at recognised institutions.
  • Journalist Visa (J): For journalists and media personnel.
  • Intern Visa (IN): For foreign nationals undertaking internship with Indian organisations.
  • Film Visa (F): For foreign nationals involved in film shooting in India.
  • Missionary Visa (M): For religious/missionary activities.

Apply for regular visas through the official portal at indianvisaonline.gov.in (select "Regular/Paper visa by Indian Mission/Post").

e-Visa vs Regular Visa — Comparison

Feature e-Visa Regular (Sticker) Visa
Application Method100% online at indianvisaonline.gov.inOnline form + in-person submission at Indian Mission/Post or VFS Global/BLS centre
Eligible Countries166+ countries/territories (check indianvisaonline.gov.in for current list)All countries (subject to specific restrictions)
Entry PointsDesignated airports and seaports onlyAny authorised Immigration Check Post
Processing TimeUp to 72 hours (per Bureau of Immigration); apply at least 4 days before arrivalMinimum 3 working days (varies by Mission)
Extensible?No — non-extendable (except e-Medical in some cases)Yes — through FRRO/FRO
Convertible?No — non-convertiblePossible in certain circumstances via FRRO
Embassy Visit Required?NoYes (or authorised VFS/BLS centre)
Protected/Restricted AreasNot valid — separate permit requiredSeparate Restricted Area Permit (RAP) required

e-Visa Eligible Countries

According to the Bureau of Immigration (indianvisaonline.gov.in), citizens of over 166 countries and territories are currently eligible for India e-Visa. These include nationals of the United States, United Kingdom, all European Union member states, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman, South Africa, Japan, South Korea, Singapore, and many more.

The complete and up-to-date list of eligible countries is published on the official portal at indianvisaonline.gov.in/evisa. The list is subject to change by the Government of India. Nationals of countries not on the eligible list must apply for a regular visa at the nearest Indian Mission or Post.

🚫 Important Restriction — Pakistani Nationals

Pakistani nationals are not eligible for the India e-Visa scheme. Additionally, per the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in), as of 27 April 2025, all existing valid visas (except medical visas, long-term visas, and diplomatic/official visas) issued to Pakistani nationals were revoked. Visa and immigration rules for Pakistani nationals are subject to specific government policy — verify directly with the nearest Indian Mission before travel.

Visa on Arrival — Japan, South Korea & UAE

Per the official indianvisaonline.gov.in portal, Visa on Arrival is available at selected Indian airports for nationals of:

  • Japan
  • South Korea
  • UAE — only for UAE nationals who have previously obtained an e-Visa or regular/paper visa for India

Japanese nationals are required to fill an application form and approach the "Visa Counter" at the designated airport on arrival, per Ministry of Home Affairs guidelines. An immigration officer then stamps the Visa on Arrival on the passport after checking eligibility criteria and payment of fee.

India e-Tourist Visa — Validity Options (2026)

Note on 180-day rule: Per indianvisaonline.gov.in, if a 1-year or 5-year e-Tourist Visa holder intends to stay for more than 180 days continuously, they are required to register with the FRRO/FRO concerned within two weeks after the expiry of 180 days.

India e-Visa Fees (2026)

Fees for India e-Visas are set in US dollars and vary by nationality and visa duration. The fee schedule is published by the Bureau of Immigration. According to official fee schedules (updated February 2026) sourced via indianvisaonline.gov.in:

Fees above are based on the Bureau of Immigration official e-Visa fee schedule (updated 20 February 2026). Certain nationalities pay different rates — for example, Japan and Sri Lanka pay USD 25 flat for 30-day, 1-year, and 5-year visas; South Africa's 30-day visa is free. Always verify the exact fee for your nationality at the official portal indianvisaonline.gov.in/evisa before applying, as fees are country-specific and non-refundable.

India e-Visa Photo Requirements (2026)

Uploading the correct photograph is mandatory for all India e-Visa applications submitted through indianvisaonline.gov.in. An incorrect photo is one of the most common reasons for application delays and rejections. The following specifications are sourced directly from the official Indian Visa Online portal's photo requirements page (indianvisaonline.gov.in/visa/instruction.html and indianvisaonline.gov.in/visa/VSS_IMAGE.pdf).

⚠️ India Visa Photo vs Indian Passport Photo — These Are Different

India visa photos are square (51×51mm / 2×2 inch). Indian passport photos for the Passport Seva portal are rectangular (35×45mm). These are two entirely different specifications. Do not use an Indian passport photo for a visa application, or vice versa.

Common India Visa Photo Rejection Reasons

  • Photo is not square (rectangular photos are rejected)
  • Face not centred or head tilted
  • Eyes not looking directly at the camera
  • Shadows on face or background
  • Glasses present (especially for e-Visa applications)
  • Photo taken as a selfie
  • File size exceeds the limit (over 1 MB for e-Visa, or 300 KB for regular visa)
  • Non-JPEG file format (PNG, HEIC, PDF not accepted)
  • Photo older than 6 months
  • Background not plain white/off-white
  • Heavy editing, filters, or colour alteration applied

Required Documents for India e-Visa Application

According to the official Indian e-Visa portal (indianvisaonline.gov.in), the following documents must be submitted for an e-Visa application:

  • Valid passport: Must have at least 6 months validity from the date of arrival in India, and at least 2 blank pages for immigration stamping.
  • Colour JPEG photograph: Meeting the specifications above (2×2 inch square, white background, JPEG, 10 KB–1 MB).
  • Scanned copy of passport biographical page (data page): PDF format, maximum 300 KB, showing your personal details and photograph clearly.
  • Return/onward ticket: Proof of travel out of India.
  • Proof of sufficient funds: For your intended stay.
  • Address in India: Hotel booking or host address (required in the application form).

Additional documents by visa type (per indianvisaonline.gov.in):

  • e-Business Visa: Business card, details of the sending and receiving organisations.
  • e-Medical Visa: Letter from the recognised/specialised hospital in India confirming treatment, appointment details.
  • e-Conference Visa: Invitation letter from the organising body.

Step-by-Step India e-Visa Application Process

The following steps reflect the official application process as described on indianvisaonline.gov.in/evisa:

  1. Visit the official portal: indianvisaonline.gov.in/evisa and click "Apply here for e-Visa".
  2. Fill in the online application form with your passport details, travel plans, address in India, and personal information. Save your Temporary Application ID in case you need to return to complete the form.
  3. Upload your square JPEG photograph (10 KB–1 MB) and a scanned PDF of your passport's biographical page (max 300 KB).
  4. Pay the visa fee online using a credit or debit card, or PayPal. A banking charge applies (2.5% for cards, per official FAQs). Note: the fee is non-refundable.
  5. Await your Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) by email. Processing typically takes up to 72 hours per the Bureau of Immigration, though applicants should apply well in advance.
  6. Check your application status at indianvisaonline.gov.in. Ensure the status shows "Granted" before travelling.
  7. Print a copy of your ETA and carry it with you — it must be presented at the Immigration Check Post on arrival, where the e-Visa will be stamped on your passport.

📅 How Far in Advance to Apply

Per official guidance on indianvisaonline.gov.in: 30-day e-Tourist Visa — can be applied up to 120 days before arrival date. 1-year and 5-year e-Tourist Visas — can also be applied up to 120 days before the intended arrival date. Apply at least 4 business days before your travel date to account for processing time, as per Bureau of Immigration guidelines.

e-Arrival Card — Mandatory for All Arrivals (2026)

Per the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in and indianvisaonline.gov.in), all foreign nationals — including OCI card holders — are now required to submit an e-Arrival Card online within 72 hours before arrival in India. This is separate from the visa and is for immigration record purposes.

The e-Arrival Card can be submitted at boi.gov.in, indianvisaonline.gov.in, or through the official "Indian Visa Su-Swagatam" mobile app.

Designated Entry Points for e-Visa Holders

e-Visa holders can only enter India through designated Immigration Check Posts. Per indianvisaonline.gov.in, the following airports and seaports are designated for e-Visa entry:

Designated Airports (33 airports, per official list)

Delhi, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Chennai, Kolkata, Hyderabad, Goa (Dabolim), Goa (Mopa), Jaipur, Cochin, Ahmedabad, Amritsar, Pune, Varanasi, Trivandrum, Lucknow, Gaya, Bagdogra, Bhubaneswar, Calicut, Chandigarh, Coimbatore, Guwahati, Indore, Kannur, Madurai, Mangalore, Nagpur, Port Blair, Surat, Tiruchirapalli, Vijayawada, and Visakhapatnam.

Designated Seaports (for general e-Visa entry)

Agatti, Calicut, Chennai, Cochin, Goa, Kamarajar, Kandla, Kattupalli, Kolkata, Kollam, Mangalore, Mumbai, Mundra, Nhava Sheva, Port Blair, Vallarpadam, Visakhapatnam, Vizhinjam, and Vizhinjam International.

Note: Entry by cruise ship is allowed through 5 designated seaports: Mumbai, Chennai, Cochin, Goa (Mormugao), and New Mangalore, per the official e-Visa FAQ.

e-Visa holders can exit from any authorised Immigration Check Post in India — they are not restricted to departure from the same airport they arrived at.

FRRO Registration

Per Ministry of Home Affairs guidelines, foreign nationals staying in India for more than 180 days on a 1-year or 5-year e-Tourist Visa are required to register with the Foreigners Regional Registration Office (FRRO) or Foreigners Registration Office (FRO) within two weeks after the expiry of 180 days.

Foreign nationals on certain long-stay regular visas (such as student, employment, and research visas) may also have FRRO registration requirements. Confirm your specific obligations based on your visa type and duration of stay. FRRO services are accessible through the online portal at indianfrro.gov.in.

OCI Card — Overseas Citizen of India

Per the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in), the OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) Card is a lifelong multiple-entry visa issued to a person who is a citizen of another country but who, or whose parents, grandparents, or great-grandparents, was a citizen of India at the time of, or at any time after, the commencement of the Constitution of India.

An OCI card is not a visa in the traditional sense, but functions as a permanent travel document for entry into India without requiring a separate visa. OCI holders are, however, still required to submit the e-Arrival Card before arriving in India.

Special Rules for Specific Nationalities

Nepal and Bhutan Nationals

Citizens of Nepal and Bhutan do not require a visa to enter India. However, if entering from China, Macau, Hong Kong, or Pakistan, a passport and visa are required. (Source: Bureau of Immigration, boi.gov.in)

Maldivian Nationals

Maldivian citizens may visit India visa-free for up to 90 days for tourism with a valid Maldivian passport, per the Incredible India government portal.

Pakistani Nationals

Pakistani nationals are not eligible for the India e-Visa. Per Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in), effective 27 April 2025, all valid visas (except medical visas, long-term visas, and diplomatic/official visas) issued to Pakistani nationals were revoked. Persons of Pakistani origin (where the person or either parent or grandparent was born in or permanently resident in Pakistan) are also excluded from the e-Visa scheme. Always check with the nearest Indian Mission for current rules.

Dual Citizens

India does not permit dual citizenship. Indian-born individuals who have acquired foreign citizenship must use their foreign passport to enter India. OCI card holders use their foreign passport in conjunction with their OCI card.

Yellow Fever Vaccination Requirement

Per the official e-Visa FAQ on indianvisaonline.gov.in: all foreigners arriving in India within 6 days of departure from any Yellow Fever endemic country are required to possess (in original) a valid Yellow Fever vaccination certificate at the time of arrival. The list of endemic countries is published by the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare at mohfw.gov.in.

Visa Extension and Conversion

Per the Bureau of Immigration:

  • e-Visa is non-extendable in most cases. Extension of e-Medical Visa may be granted up to 6 months on a case-by-case basis by the FRRO/FRO.
  • e-Visa is non-convertible — it cannot be converted to any other visa category.
  • Extension of regular visas may be granted by the FRRO/FRO. Any further extension beyond what FRRO can grant must be approved by the Ministry of Home Affairs.

Overstay Penalties

Overstaying your Indian visa is a serious immigration violation. Foreign nationals who overstay are subject to penalties, deportation, and potential bans from future entry into India. Per the Bureau of Immigration, foreign nationals in India must comply with the terms and conditions of their visa including duration of stay. If you need to extend your stay, contact your jurisdictional FRRO/FRO before your visa expires.

Conference Clearance for Events (New — 2026)

Per the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in), a new online portal has been launched at conference.epolclearance.gov.in — the Conference Clearance System — for expeditious processing of political clearances for foreign nationals participating in conferences, seminars, workshops, and sports events organised in India by Government/autonomous institutions/private bodies.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Q: Can I apply for an India e-Visa if my passport has less than 6 months validity?

No. Per indianvisaonline.gov.in, your passport must have at least 6 months validity from the date of arrival in India to be eligible for an e-Visa. You must also have at least 2 blank pages available in your passport.

Q: Can I visit Restricted or Protected Areas on an India e-Visa?

No. Per the Bureau of Immigration, e-Visa is not valid for visiting Protected Areas, Restricted Areas, or Cantonment Areas. A separate permit from the relevant civil authority is required. Some areas such as Arunachal Pradesh, Sikkim border zones, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, and Lakshadweep require separate permits — check with the Indian Mission before travel.

Q: Is the India e-Visa fee refundable if my visa is rejected?

No. Per the official FAQ on indianvisaonline.gov.in: "e-Visa processing fee once submitted is non-refundable as the fee is for processing of the application and is not dependent on either grant or rejection of the application."

Q: Can I enter India by cruise ship on an e-Visa?

Yes. Per indianvisaonline.gov.in, entry by cruise ship is permitted through 5 designated seaports: Mumbai, Chennai, Cochin, Goa (Mormugao), and New Mangalore.

Q: Do I need to submit an e-Arrival Card even if I have a regular visa?

Yes. Per the Bureau of Immigration (boi.gov.in), all foreign nationals — including OCI card holders — must submit the e-Arrival Card online within 72 hours before arrival in India, regardless of visa type. This can be done at boi.gov.in, indianvisaonline.gov.in, or via the official "Indian Visa Su-Swagatam" mobile app.

Q: How do I check my India e-Visa application status?

You can check your application status at indianvisaonline.gov.in. Ensure the status shows "Granted" before travelling. Your ETA will also be sent to your registered email address.

description: Official 2026 guide to Germany student visa photo requirements. Verified against the Federal Foreign Office (Auswärtiges Amt), Federal Ministry of the Interior (BMI), and ICAO Document 9303.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
Germany Student Visa Photo Requirements (2026 Complete Guide): Size, Background, Biometric Rules & Common Mistakes

Applying for a Germany student visa (Visum zu Studienzwecken) requires submitting biometric photographs that meet strict official standards. A photo that does not comply with these standards is one of the most common reasons for visa application delays and rejections. This guide covers every official requirement for your Germany student visa photo in 2026 — size, face coverage, background, resolution, expression, glasses, head coverings, and how many photos you need to submit.

All specifications in this guide are sourced from the Federal Foreign Office of Germany (Auswärtiges Amt), the Federal Ministry of the Interior and Community (Bundesministerium des Innern und für Heimat, BMI), and the International Civil Aviation Organisation (ICAO) Document 9303, which Germany's biometric photo rules are based on. Always verify the latest requirements with your local German Mission or Consulate before submitting your application at auswaertiges-amt.de.

Key Facts at a Glance

  • Photo size: 35 mm (width) x 45 mm (height)
  • Face coverage: 70–80% of the photo (32–36 mm from chin to crown)
  • Background: Plain light grey or white — must contrast with face and hair
  • Number of photos: 2 identical recent photos for most German Missions
  • Age of photo: Taken within the last 6 months
  • Resolution: Minimum 600 DPI (827 x 1063 pixels)
  • Format: Colour photograph only — no black and white
  • Glasses: Not permitted

Who Needs a Germany Student Visa?

According to the Federal Foreign Office (Auswärtiges Amt), citizens of non-EU/EEA countries who intend to study in Germany for more than 90 days must obtain a national visa (Category D) before travelling. There are two types relevant to students:

  • Student Visa (Visum zu Studienzwecken): For applicants who have already received a formal admission letter from a German university or higher education institution.
  • Student Applicant Visa (Visum zur Studienbewerbung): For applicants still in the process of applying to German universities. Valid for up to 9 months. Must be converted to a residence permit after arrival.

Both visa categories require the same biometric photograph standards as set out below. Citizens of EU, EEA, and Switzerland do not require a visa to study in Germany.

Official Germany Student Visa Photo Size Requirements

Germany's visa photo specifications are defined by the Federal Ministry of the Interior (BMI) and aligned with ICAO Document 9303 biometric standards, as applied by the Federal Foreign Office across all German Missions worldwide.

Dimensions

  • Width: 35 mm
  • Height: 45 mm
  • Face height (chin to crown of head): 32–36 mm — this ensures the face occupies 70–80% of the total photo area.
Specification Official Requirement
Photo width35 mm
Photo height45 mm
Face height (chin to crown)32–36 mm (70–80% of frame)
Minimum print resolution600 DPI (827 × 1063 pixels)
ColourColour only (no black and white)
Age of photographWithin the last 6 months
Number of photos required2 identical photos (standard; varies by Mission)
Print finishMatte or semi-matte (not glossy)

Background Requirements

According to the official photo sample guidelines published by German authorities and aligned with ICAO Document 9303 standards:

  • The background must be plain and uniform — no patterns, gradients, or objects in the background.
  • The official German biometric photo sample specifies a neutral light grey background. A plain white background is also accepted, provided it provides sufficient contrast with the applicant's face and hair.
  • For applicants with light or blonde hair, a medium grey background is recommended to ensure adequate contrast.
  • For applicants with dark hair, a light grey or white background is recommended.
  • No shadows should appear on the background or on the applicant's face.

Note on Background Colour

The Federal Ministry of the Interior's official biometric photo template specifies a neutral grey background, not pure white. While white is accepted at many German Missions, the preferred standard per published German authority templates is light grey. Check with your specific German Embassy or Consulate for their local preference before having your photo taken.

Face and Expression Requirements

Germany's student visa photo must meet the following face and expression requirements, per ICAO Document 9303 as applied by the Federal Foreign Office:

  • Facing directly forward — no tilting, turning, or angling of the head.
  • Neutral expression — mouth closed, neither smiling nor frowning.
  • Both eyes open and clearly visible — eyes must not be closed, half-closed, or obscured.
  • Forehead and full crown of head fully visible — the top of the head must not be cut off.
  • Chin fully visible — the chin must not be cut off at the bottom of the frame.
  • Even, shadow-free illumination — no shadows on the face or behind the head. Over-exposure and under-exposure must be avoided.
  • Natural skin tone and eye colour — colour balance must represent the applicant accurately.
  • The image must be sharp, in focus, and not blurred.

Glasses Policy

Per the updated guidance from German authorities aligned with current biometric standards:

  • Glasses are not permitted in Germany visa and passport photos.
  • This applies to all types of eyewear, including prescription glasses, tinted glasses, and sunglasses.
  • If you normally wear glasses, you must remove them for your visa photo.

Head Coverings

  • Head coverings are not permitted in Germany visa photos except where worn for documented religious reasons.
  • If a head covering is worn for religious reasons, it must not obscure any part of the face — the full face from chin to forehead must be clearly visible.
  • Hats, caps, or fashion headwear are not permitted under any circumstances.

How Many Photos Are Required for a Germany Student Visa?

According to standard requirements across German Missions and confirmed by the Federal Foreign Office:

  • Most German Embassies and Consulates require 2 identical biometric photographs for a national visa (Category D) application, which includes the student visa.
  • Both photos must be identical — taken in the same session, meeting all the specifications above.
  • Some German Missions may require a different number of photos. For example, German Missions in India require 3 photos not older than 6 months, while the German Embassy in Georgia requires 2 photos not older than 1 year.

Check Your Local German Mission

Photo quantity requirements can vary by country. Always verify the exact number of photos required with the German Embassy or Consulate responsible for your country of residence before submitting your application. The official directory of German Missions is at auswaertiges-amt.de.

Digital Photo Submissions

Germany's domestic document workflow changed from 1 May 2025, when the Federal Ministry of the Interior introduced digital-only photo submission for passport and national ID card applications processed inside Germany. However, for visa applications submitted at German Missions abroad — which includes student visa applications — both digital photo uploads and printed photos continue to be accepted depending on the consulate's online application portal requirements. The same biometric specifications (35x45mm, 600 DPI minimum, 70–80% face coverage) apply to digital submissions.

Common Reasons for Photo Rejection

The following are the most frequent reasons Germany student visa photos are rejected by German Missions:

  • Photo is older than 6 months
  • Face coverage is below 70% or above 80% of the photo area
  • Background is not plain — patterned, dark, or has shadows
  • Photo is blurred, underexposed, or overexposed
  • Glasses are present in the photo
  • Head is tilted or turned to one side
  • The top of the head or chin is cut off
  • Shadows are visible on the face or background
  • Photo has been digitally altered to change facial features
  • Photo is printed on glossy paper rather than matte or semi-matte
  • Resolution is below 600 DPI (photo appears pixelated or unclear)

Germany Student Visa Photo Requirements vs. Other Common Visa Formats

Feature Germany Student Visa India e-Visa US Visa / Passport

Face coverage70–80%50% minimum50–69%

GlassesNot permittedNot permittedNot permitted

Frequently Asked Questions

Q: Can I use the same photo for my Germany student visa and my residence permit application in Germany?

The biometric photo specifications (35x45mm, 70–80% face coverage, light background, 600 DPI) are the same for both the student visa application abroad and the residence permit (Aufenthaltstitel) application at the local Ausländerbehörde in Germany. However, your visa photo must be no older than 6 months at the time of each separate application. If significant time has passed between your visa application and your residence permit application, a new photo will be required.

Q: Does Germany require a white or grey background for visa photos?

Both are referenced across official sources. The Federal Ministry of the Interior's official biometric photo template specifies a light grey background. White backgrounds are accepted at most German Missions provided there is adequate contrast between the background and the applicant's face and hair. The key requirement per ICAO Document 9303 — which Germany follows — is that the background must be plain, uniform, and contrasting with the face. When in doubt, use a neutral light grey.

Q: Are photos taken on a smartphone accepted for the Germany student visa?

German Missions accept printed photos taken with a smartphone provided the final printed image meets all biometric specifications: 35x45mm, minimum 600 DPI print resolution, correct face coverage, plain background, sharp focus, and no shadows. The photo must be printed on matte or semi-matte photo paper, not on standard printer paper. For digital submission through an online visa portal, the image file must meet the resolution requirements set by that specific portal.

Q: Is the Germany student visa photo the same as a Schengen visa photo?

The Germany student visa is a national visa (Category D), not a Schengen visa (Category C). However, both use the same 35x45mm biometric photo specification as defined by the Federal Foreign Office and aligned with the Schengen Visa Code (Regulation (EC) No 810/2009) and ICAO Document 9303.

Q: How many photos do I need for a Germany student visa application in India?

According to published requirements from German Missions in India, applicants applying from India are required to submit 3 biometric photos not older than 6 months. This differs from the standard 2-photo requirement at many other German Missions. Always verify current requirements directly with your nearest German Embassy or Consulate in India at india.diplo.de before your appointment.

Q: Can I wear religious head coverings in my Germany student visa photo?

Yes, head coverings worn for documented religious reasons are permitted. However, the covering must not obscure any part of the face — the full face from chin to forehead must be completely and clearly visible. No shadow may fall on the face as a result of the head covering. Hats or fashion headwear are not permitted under any circumstances.

RecencyTaken within the last 1 month (adults)
GlassesNot permitted (banned since 2021 in line with ICAO)
ExpressionNeutral, mouth closed, eyes open and forward-facing
HeadwearNot permitted except religious/medical exemptions
Paper typePlain colour photographic paper, professionally printed
Photo editingNot permitted — no retouching, filters, or enhancements
Issuing authorityUK Visas and Immigration (UKVI), Home Office

UK Visa Photo Size: Exact Dimensions

The required print size for all UK visa photographs is 35mm wide by 45mm tall — a portrait-orientation rectangle, not a square. This is identical to the UK passport photo size and is set by UKVI in line with ICAO biometric standards.

Head Height Requirements

Within that 45mm height, the distance from your chin to the top of your head (crown, not hair) must measure between 29mm and 34mm. This equates to approximately 64–76% of the total photo height. A head that is too large or too small is one of the most common reasons UKVI rejects photos at biometric appointment centres.

Digital Photo Pixel Dimensions

  • At 300 DPI: 413 × 531 pixels (minimum acceptable for digital submission)
  • At 600 DPI: 827 × 1063 pixels (recommended for highest quality)
  • File format: JPEG or PNG
  • File size: Between 50 KB and 10 MB

Background Colour: Light Grey or Cream — Not White

This is the single most misunderstood UK visa photo requirement, and the cause of a large proportion of rejections. The background must be plain light grey or cream — pure white is not acceptable.

Why Does UKVI Reject White Backgrounds?

The automated biometric face-matching system used at UKVI application centres is calibrated against a neutral off-white tone. A pure white background can create overexposure artefacts — especially around the hairline and ears — that interfere with automated facial geometry checks. Applicants who arrive at their biometric appointment with a white-background photo are commonly sent back to a photo booth, causing delays.

Acceptable background colours include:

  • Light grey (pale neutral grey, not dark grey)
  • Cream (warm off-white)
  • Plain off-white with no patterns, shadows, or gradients

Background colours that will be rejected:

  • Pure white (RGB 255,255,255)
  • Coloured backgrounds (blue, yellow, green, etc.)
  • Patterned, textured, or shadowed backgrounds
  • Backgrounds where the face blends into the background

Face and Expression Requirements

UKVI biometric rules require your face to be clearly and neutrally presented so that automated and manual identity checks can be carried out accurately.

Facial Expression

  • Expression: Neutral — relaxed face, mouth closed
  • Eyes: Open, clearly visible, looking directly at the camera
  • Head position: Facing straight forward — no tilting, turning, or angling
  • No smiling, frowning, or raised eyebrows
  • Both ears should be visible where possible
  • Hair must not cover the eyes or eyebrows

Lighting and Focus

  • Photo must be in sharp focus — no blurring
  • Even lighting across the face — no shadows on the face or background
  • No red-eye effect
  • No flash shadows behind the head
  • Natural daylight or two balanced lamps at equal distance from both sides of the face is recommended

Glasses Rules: No Glasses Permitted (Since 2021)

Glasses of any kind are not permitted in UK visa photographs as of 2021. This includes prescription glasses, reading glasses, and fashion frames. The UK implemented a full ban on glasses in line with updated ICAO guidance adopted by most major immigration authorities globally. Contact lenses are permitted as they are invisible in photographs.

Glasses that are always rejected:

  • Prescription glasses (any frame style)
  • Reading glasses
  • Sunglasses or tinted lenses
  • Transition or photochromic lenses
  • Fashion frames with clear lenses

Headwear Rules

Hats, caps, beanies, and all casual headwear are not permitted. Religious and medical exemptions apply under the following strict conditions:

Religious Head Coverings (Permitted with Conditions)

  • Permitted: hijab, turban, kippah, and other daily religious headwear
  • Full face must be visible from chin to top of forehead
  • Both sides of the face must be clearly visible from ear to ear
  • The covering must not cast any shadow on the face
  • Eyebrows, eyes, nose, mouth, and chin must all be clearly visible

Medical Head Coverings

  • Permitted for documented medical reasons
  • A signed supporting letter may be required
  • Same face visibility conditions apply as for religious coverings

Photo Print Requirements

Number of Photos Required

For paper-based UK visa applications, you must submit 2 identical photographs, unattached and loose (not stapled or glued). Both copies must be identical — printed from the same source at the same time.

Print Quality

  • Professionally printed on plain colour photographic paper
  • In colour — black and white photos are not accepted
  • Not creased, torn, marked, or bent
  • Not printed on ordinary office paper
  • No digital alterations — no blemish removal, skin tone adjustment, or other post-processing

Writing on the Back of the Photo

Where required by your specific application form, write your full name and date of birth lightly on the back of one photo using a pencil (not pen, which can bleed through).

UK Visa Photo Requirements by Visa Category

UKVI applies the same 35 × 45mm biometric photo specification across all visa categories. The table below confirms this for the most common visa routes:

Visa Category Photo Size Same UKVI Standard?
Standard Visitor Visa35 × 45mm✅ Yes
Student Visa (formerly Tier 4)35 × 45mm✅ Yes
Skilled Worker Visa35 × 45mm✅ Yes
Family Visa (Spouse/Partner)35 × 45mm✅ Yes
Graduate Visa35 × 45mm✅ Yes
Health and Care Worker Visa35 × 45mm✅ Yes
Indefinite Leave to Remain (ILR)35 × 45mm✅ Yes

Child and Infant Photo Rules

Children's photos follow the same background, size, and lighting requirements as adults, with the following relaxed rules:

  • Babies under 1 year: Eyes do not need to be open
  • Children under 6 years: Do not need to be looking directly at the lens, but must be facing forward
  • No toys, dummies, or hands should appear in the photo
  • No parent's hands or other people should be visible
  • The child must appear alone in the photo with a clear background
  • A supporting hand may be used to keep an infant in position, but must not be visible in the final image

Top Reasons UK Visa Photos Are Rejected

UKVI automated and manual checks at biometric appointment centres consistently reject photos for the following reasons. Review this list before submitting your application:

Rejection Reason How to Fix It
Pure white backgroundRetake against a light grey or cream background
Glasses wornRemove all glasses — no exceptions
Head too large or too smallEnsure chin-to-crown = 29–34mm within the 45mm height
Shadows on face or backgroundUse even, diffuse lighting from both sides
Smiling or open mouthAdopt a neutral expression, mouth firmly closed
Hair covering eyes or eyebrowsTie hair back or push it behind the ears
Photo older than 1 monthRetake the photo — UKVI recency rules are strictly enforced
Blurry or out-of-focus imageUse rear camera on a stand; avoid front selfie camera
Photo edited or filteredDo not apply any post-processing; submit the original
Red-eye effectUse natural light or indirect flash; use red-eye reduction setting

How to Take a Compliant UK Visa Photo at Home (Step-by-Step)

You do not need a professional photo studio. A compliant UK visa photo can be taken at home following these steps:

  1. Choose your background. Stand in front of a plain off-white, cream, or light grey wall. Avoid patterned wallpaper. Do not use a white wall — it will likely appear pure white.
  2. Set up lighting. Use natural daylight from a window facing you, or two lamps positioned at equal distances on either side. Avoid single-direction lighting that creates shadows.
  3. Remove glasses and headwear. Remove all glasses. Remove all headwear unless worn for documented religious or medical reasons.
  4. Position your camera at eye level. Use a phone stand or ask someone to hold the camera. Position the camera 1–1.5 metres away. Do not use the selfie (front-facing) camera — use the rear camera for higher resolution.
  5. Adopt the correct expression. Look directly into the camera. Keep your mouth closed and your face relaxed. Both eyes must be fully open.
  6. Check the framing. Your full face, top of the head (with a small gap above), and the top of your shoulders should all be visible. Verify your chin-to-crown head height will fall within 29–34mm once printed at 35×45mm.
  7. Verify and resize. Use a UKVI-compliant tool to resize the photo to exactly 35×45mm at 300 DPI, confirm the background colour is correct, and check no shadows are present before printing.

Where to Print Your UK Visa Photo in 2026

You can have your UK visa photo professionally printed at the following locations in the UK:

  • Boots Pharmacy — photo kiosk and counter service, approximately £8–10 for 2 photos
  • Snappy Snaps — professional print service available at most branches
  • Post Offices — photo booth and counter service at selected branches
  • Tesco and Asda photo kiosks — self-service print from a prepared digital file
  • Independent photo studios — most offer an official biometric photo service

If you have already prepared a compliant digital file (correctly cropped to 35×45mm at 300 DPI), you can use a self-service kiosk to print — this is typically faster and cheaper than the counter service.

UK Visa Photo vs UK Passport Photo: Key Differences

Requirement UK Visa (UKVI) UK Passport (HMPO)
Photo size35 × 45mm35 × 45mm
BackgroundLight grey or creamLight grey or cream
Head height29–34mm29–34mm
RecencyWithin 1 monthWithin 1 month (renewal)
Issuing authorityUKVI (Home Office)HMPO
Countersignature needed?RarelySometimes (first-time, child)
Cross-use acceptable?✅ A UKVI-spec photo satisfies HMPO⚠️ Some HMPO photos fail UKVI's stricter background check

⚠️ Important Note on Cross-Use

A photo that satisfies the UKVI visa specification will generally also satisfy HMPO passport requirements. However, the reverse is not guaranteed — some photos that pass HMPO checks carry a background tone that UKVI's automated system flags. If in doubt, prepare a dedicated photo for your visa application.

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Can I wear glasses in my UK visa photo in 2026?

No. Glasses of any kind — including prescription glasses and reading glasses — are not permitted in UK visa photographs. This ban has been in place since 2021 in line with updated ICAO guidance. Contact lenses are permitted as they are not visible in photographs.

Does the UK visa photo background have to be white?

No. This is a very common mistake. The UK visa photo background must be light grey or cream — pure white is specifically not acceptable. A white background is one of the leading causes of rejection at UKVI biometric appointments.

How old can my UK visa photo be?

Your UK visa photo must have been taken within the last one month for adult applicants. Using an older photo — even one taken 6–12 weeks ago — is a grounds for rejection.

How many photos do I need for a UK visa application?

Paper-based applications require 2 identical photographs, unattached and loose. Online applications via the UKVI portal or ID Check app use a digital upload — no prints are required for those routes.

Can I use my UK passport photo for my UK visa application?

A UKVI-standard visa photo will generally satisfy HMPO passport requirements. However, some passport photos may fail UKVI's stricter background tone check. It is safest to prepare a dedicated photo confirmed against UKVI specifications.

Can I wear a hijab or religious head covering in my UK visa photo?

Yes, provided the head covering is worn daily for genuine religious reasons. Your full face from chin to top of forehead — and both sides from ear to ear — must be completely visible. The covering must not cast any shadow on your face.

What is the correct UK visa photo size in pixels?

At 300 DPI (minimum acceptable), a 35×45mm photo is 413 × 531 pixels. At 600 DPI (recommended), it is 827 × 1063 pixels. The JPEG file must be between 50 KB and 10 MB.

Can I take my UK visa photo on my phone?

Yes. Use the rear (main) camera — not the front selfie camera — at 1–1.5 metres distance on a stand. Ensure even lighting, a light grey or cream background, and verify the image is in sharp focus before printing or uploading. A compliant tool can then resize and verify the result before submission.

Do I need to print my UK visa photo if I apply online?

No. Online applications via the UKVI portal or the ID Check app require a digital photo upload only. You cannot mix formats — online applicants use digital photos exclusively.

content:
UK Digital Passport Photo: How to Take & Submit Online (2026)

If you're applying for a British passport through the online service, you'll need a UK digital passport photo rather than a printed one. HM Passport Office (HMPO) only accepts digital photos for online applications, and the file has to pass a specific set of technical and presentation rules before your application can move forward. This guide pulls the requirements straight from gov.uk so you know exactly what "compliant" looks like before you upload anything.

What Counts as a UK Digital Passport Photo?

A UK digital passport photo is the JPEG image you upload when you renew or apply for a passport through the gov.uk online service. It's different from the printed 45mm × 35mm photo used on paper applications — there's no millimetre measurement to worry about, but the pixel dimensions, file size, and image quality all have to meet HMPO's published standards under gov.uk digital passport photo rules.

UK Digital Passport Photo Requirements (Official HMPO Rules)

According to gov.uk, every digital photo submitted with an online passport application must meet these UK digital passport photo requirements:

  • At least 413 pixels wide and 531 pixels tall
  • Between 50KB and 10MB in file size
  • Clear, in focus, and in colour
  • Unaltered by computer software or filters
  • Taken within the last month
  • Shot against a plain, light-coloured background with clear contrast between you and the background
  • Free of "red eye," shadows on your face or behind you, and any other people or objects in frame

On top of the technical checks, these British digital photo requirements also cover how you appear in the shot. You must be facing forward and looking straight at the camera, have a neutral expression with your mouth closed, keep both eyes open and visible, and avoid hair falling across your eyes. Head coverings aren't permitted unless worn for religious or medical reasons, and glasses should be left off unless you need them — if you do wear them, the lenses must be clear with no glare, reflection, or frame shadow over your eyes.

Official HMPO digital photo example. Source: gov.uk

Digital vs Printed: Quick Comparison

Feature Digital Photo (Online) Printed Photo (Paper Form)
Minimum size 413 × 531 px 45mm high × 35mm wide
File rules JPEG, 50KB–10MB Glossy/matte paper, no border, no creases
Head height Cropped automatically by the system 29mm–34mm, crown to chin
Background Plain, light-coloured Plain cream or light grey
Quantity 1 digital upload 2 identical prints

How to Take Your Own UK Digital Passport Photo at Home

You can take the photo yourself on a phone or camera during the online application, though gov.uk notes that photos from a booth or shop are more likely to be approved than ones taken on your own device. If you're doing it yourself:

  • Stand facing a plain, light-coloured wall in even, natural light — avoid harsh overhead lighting that casts shadows
  • Ask someone else to take the photo for you; HMPO requires help from another person for self-submitted shots
  • Include your head, shoulders, and upper body in the frame — don't pre-crop the image, as the system crops it for you
  • Remove hats, scarves, or headphones unless worn for religious or medical reasons
  • Keep a neutral expression, mouth closed, eyes open and clearly visible

HMPO framing guidance for head, shoulders, and upper body. Source: gov.uk

Two Ways to Get a Compliant Digital Photo

Option 1: Photo Code From a Booth or Shop

Before starting your application, visit a photo booth or shop and choose the option to receive a code with your prints. You'll enter that code during your passport application, and the digital photo on file is added automatically.

Option 2: Upload a Photo From Your Own Device

If you'd rather skip the shop, you can take and upload a photo directly during the application. The gov.uk online service runs an automated check on the image as part of the UK passport digital photo checker process built into the application itself, flagging anything that doesn't meet the rules above so you can retake it before submitting.

Digital Photo Rules for Babies and Children

  • Children must be photographed alone, with no toys or dummies in shot
  • Children under 6 don't need to look directly at the camera or hold a plain expression
  • Babies under 1 don't need their eyes open; a hand can support their head as long as it's out of frame
  • Babies under 1 should lie on a plain, light-coloured sheet, photographed from above

Official HMPO digital photo example for children. Source: gov.uk

Common Reasons UK Digital Photos Get Rejected

  • Image below the 413 × 531 px pixel minimum or too heavily compressed
  • Background that isn't plain or light-coloured (patterned, dark, or cluttered)
  • Shadows across the face or behind the subject
  • Glare or reflection on glasses lenses, or frames covering the eyes
  • Smiling, an open mouth, or a non-neutral expression
  • Photo older than one month
  • Head covering not worn for religious or medical reasons

FAQs

Can I use a selfie for my UK digital passport photo?

Yes — gov.uk allows photos taken on your own device during the application, though it notes booth or shop photos are more likely to be approved on the first try.

What's the minimum pixel size for a UK digital passport photo?

413 pixels wide by 531 pixels tall, as a JPEG between 50KB and 10MB.

Do I need to crop the photo myself before uploading?

No. Include your head, shoulders, and upper body, and gov.uk's system handles the cropping automatically.

How recent does the photo need to be?

It must have been taken within the last month, even if your appearance hasn't changed.

Check Your UK Digital Passport Photo Before You Upload

Verify your photo against HMPO's 2026 digital rules: 413 × 531 px minimum, plain light background, no red-eye, correct framing — before it slows down your gov.uk application.
